learning_objectives: Allocate time-critical inference code, active frame buffers, model weights, and biometric templates to the correct memory regions based on latency, capacity, and security requirements
Explain why XSPI memory-mapped mode is the correct placement for neural network model weights that exceed on-chip SRAM capacity
Identify which memory regions support DMA access for frame buffer transfers and which are restricted to CPU-only access

content.hook_sentence: Placing a 1.5 MB camera frame buffer in DTCM RAM is a one-line mistake that silently corrupts your entire pipeline — because DTCM is only 128 Kbytes.
content.introduction: Building on the memory hierarchy established in §2.1.1, this section moves from knowing what each region is to deciding what goes where. For a face recognition pipeline, every allocation decision has a direct consequence: wrong placement means either a latency spike that breaks real-time matching or a capacity overflow that crashes the system at runtime. You will leave this section with a concrete allocation table you can apply directly to your linker script and memory layout plan.

key_points.point: ITCM is the only correct home for time-critical inference control code that runs on every captured frame.
key_points.explanation: Building on the TCM zero-wait-state guarantee from §2.1.1, the practical implication for your pipeline is this: any code that executes on every frame — your inference dispatch loop, ISR handlers that signal frame-ready events, and the real-time control logic coordinating the camera-to-NPU handoff — belongs in ITCM. The Cortex-M55 fetch path is directly coupled to ITCM, so instruction fetches never stall waiting for a cache fill or bus arbitration. AXISRAM, by contrast, is accessed through the AXI interconnect and relies on ICACHE to hide latency; a cache miss on a hot code path adds unpredictable cycles. The 64-Kbyte ITCM capacity is tight, so reserve it for the smallest, most latency-sensitive routines and keep larger, less time-critical code in AXISRAM where ICACHE can serve it efficiently.

references.snippet: 64-Kbyte of instruction TCM RAM with ECC for critical real-time routines (TCMs case not extended)
128-Kbyte TCM (tightly-coupled memory) RAM with ECC for critical real-time data + 64-Kbyte instruction TCM RAM with ECC for critical real-time routines

key_points.point: Active camera frame buffers belong in AXISRAM, not DTCM — the size mismatch alone makes DTCM impossible.
key_points.explanation: A raw Bayer frame at 1080p is on the order of 1.5 MB. DTCM is 128 Kbytes. The arithmetic ends the discussion before it starts. AXISRAM is the correct destination: the STM32N6 provides 4.2 Mbytes of contiguous on-chip SRAM, giving you enough headroom for multiple frame buffers without spilling to external memory. DTCM is CPU-private tightly coupled memory optimized for scalar data used by real-time control loops — stack variables, ISR context, small lookup tables. It is not a DMA target for bulk pixel data. When the HPDMA or DCMIPP pixel pipeline writes captured frames, the destination must be a region accessible on the AXI interconnect, which AXISRAM satisfies. Placing frame buffers in AXISRAM also keeps them directly reachable by the NPU's AXI controller ports for the ISP-to-NPU handoff.

references.snippet: The devices embed a 4.2-Mbyte contiguous SRAM organized in several banks, an 8-Kbyte backup SRAM active in V_BAT mode, and a flexible external memory controller (FMC) for static memories, XSPI 8-/16-bit configurations.
128-Kbyte data TCM RAM with ECC for critical real-time data, and 64-Kbyte of instruction TCM RAM with ECC for critical real-time routines
4.2-Mbyte contiguous SRAM

key_points.point: Neural network model weights that exceed on-chip SRAM capacity go in XSPI-mapped external memory, accessed in memory-mapped mode.
key_points.explanation: Face recognition models are typically several megabytes to tens of megabytes. The STM32N6's 4.2 MB on-chip SRAM ceiling means any model that exceeds what fits alongside frame buffers and working data must live in external memory. The XSPI interface solves this cleanly: in memory-mapped mode, the external serial memory (NOR flash, HyperRAM, or HyperFlash) appears to the system as if it were internal memory, supporting both read and write operations. The NPU's streaming engines and on-the-fly weight decompression are designed to consume weights from this kind of memory-mapped region without requiring the CPU to stage data manually. XSPI supports single, dual, quad, and octal communication, plus SDR and DTR modes, so you can tune bandwidth to match the NPU's weight fetch rate. The key constraint: XSPI-mapped memory has higher and less deterministic latency than on-chip SRAM, which is why the NPU's streaming architecture exists — to prefetch and buffer weights so inference throughput is not bottlenecked by external memory access latency.

references.snippet: Memory-mapped mode: the external memory is memory mapped and it is seen by the system as if it was an internal memory, supporting both read and write operations.
Support for single, dual, quad, and octal communication... SDR (single-data rate) and DTR (double-transfer rate) support
XSPI supporting serial PSRAM, NAND, NOR, HyperRAM™/ HyperFlash™ frame formats... 2 ports: XSPIM 8- and 16-bit configuration up to 200 MHz

key_points.point: Biometric templates stored in external flash require MCE-encrypted XSPI regions — and this capability is variant-specific.
key_points.explanation: Encrypted facial templates at rest must be protected by inline encryption so that any read or write to the XSPI-connected flash region is automatically encrypted or decrypted on the system bus, without CPU intervention. The Memory Cipher Engine (MCE) provides exactly this: system bus inline encryption for writes and decryption for reads, operating transparently within defined address regions. The critical architectural constraint is that MCE is available only on STM32N655xx and STM32N657xx devices — the STM32N6x7xx variants that carry the Neural-ART NPU do not include MCE. This means if your design requires both NPU inference and MCE-protected biometric template storage in a single device, you face a hard variant tradeoff. When you place biometric templates in an XSPI region, you configure that region as an MCE-encrypted zone; all other XSPI regions (such as the model weight region) can run in bypass mode to avoid decryption overhead on weight fetches.

references.snippet: The memory cipher engine (MCE) defines, in a given address space, multiple regions with specific security setup (encryption). All system bus traffic going through an encrypted region is managed on-the-fly by the MCE, automatically decrypting reads and encrypting writes if authorized. This function is available only on STM32N655xx and STM32N657xx devices.
Memory-mapped mode: the external memory is memory mapped and it is seen by the system as if it was an internal memory, supporting both read and write operations.

key_points.point: The 8-Kbyte backup SRAM (BKPSRAM) serves a narrow but important role: persisting small state across power cycles in $V_{BAT}$ mode.
key_points.explanation: The STM32N6 includes an 8-Kbyte backup SRAM that remains active when the main supply is off, powered by $V_{BAT}$. For a face recognition terminal, this is the right place for small persistent state — access event counters, tamper detection flags, or a cached authentication token that must survive a power interruption without requiring a full re-authentication cycle. Do not confuse BKPSRAM with general-purpose working memory; its 8-Kbyte capacity and $V_{BAT}$-domain placement make it unsuitable for frame buffers, model weights, or any bulk data. Think of it as a small, battery-backed scratchpad for the handful of values your system needs to remember across resets.

references.snippet: 8 Kbytes of backup SRAM (BKPSRAM) active in V_BAT mode
8-Kbyte backup SRAM active in V_BAT mode

examples.prediction_prompt: You are writing the memory allocation table for the face recognition terminal. The inference engine needs: (1) a tight real-time dispatch loop that runs on every frame, (2) a 2 MB face detection model, and (3) two 1080p raw Bayer frame buffers at roughly 1.5 MB each. You have ITCM (64 KB), DTCM (128 KB), AXISRAM (4.2 MB total on-chip), and XSPI-mapped external memory available. Where does each item go, and what is the reasoning?
examples.reveal: The dispatch loop goes in ITCM: it is the smallest item and the most latency-sensitive, and ITCM's direct coupling to the Cortex-M55 fetch path guarantees zero-wait-state instruction fetches on every frame. The two frame buffers (roughly 3 MB combined) go in AXISRAM: they are too large for DTCM (128 KB) by more than an order of magnitude, and AXISRAM's 4.2 MB on-chip capacity can hold both buffers with room for working data. The 2 MB face detection model goes in XSPI-mapped external memory in memory-mapped mode: it exceeds what fits in on-chip SRAM alongside the frame buffers, and memory-mapped mode makes it addressable as if it were internal memory so the NPU's streaming engines can fetch weights directly. DTCM is left for ISR stack, real-time control variables, and small lookup tables — its intended use.
examples.hint: Start by checking whether each item physically fits in the candidate region before evaluating latency.

examples.prediction_prompt: A colleague proposes using the STM32N657xx variant for a biometric terminal that needs both NPU inference and MCE-encrypted XSPI storage for facial templates. They argue that the NPU's real-time data encryption feature can substitute for MCE on the XSPI region. Is this a valid substitution, and what is the correct assessment?
examples.reveal: This is not a valid substitution. MCE provides system bus inline encryption specifically for defined address regions in XSPI-connected external memory, operating transparently on every read and write without CPU involvement. The NPU's real-time data encryption is an internal feature of the Neural-ART accelerator designed to protect data flowing through the NPU pipeline — it does not extend to encrypting arbitrary XSPI address regions at the system bus level. Furthermore, MCE is available only on STM32N655xx and STM32N657xx devices, and the STM32N657xx does include MCE, so the variant choice here is actually correct — but the colleague's reasoning about substituting NPU encryption for MCE is wrong. The correct design uses MCE to define an encrypted region in the XSPI address space for biometric templates, and configures the model weight region as MCE bypass to avoid unnecessary decryption overhead on weight fetches.
examples.hint: Check which device variants include MCE, then consider whether the NPU encryption feature operates at the system bus level or only within the NPU subsystem.

content.summary: Map each pipeline artifact to its memory region by matching latency requirements and capacity constraints: ITCM for time-critical inference control code, AXISRAM for active frame buffers, XSPI memory-mapped mode for model weights, MCE-encrypted XSPI regions for biometric templates, and BKPSRAM only for small state that must survive power loss.

micro_checks.stem: When sizing a memory region for active camera frame buffers in the face recognition pipeline, the first constraint to check is whether the region has sufficient ___ to hold the full frame data.
micro_checks.explanation: DTCM is eliminated as a frame buffer destination not because of bus access rules but because its 128 KB capacity is far smaller than a multi-megabyte frame — capacity is the binding constraint before latency or bus topology even enter the analysis.

micro_checks.stem: Match each pipeline data type to its correct memory allocation for the STM32N657xx-based face recognition terminal:

pairs.left: Real-time inference dispatch loop (runs every frame)
pairs.right: ITCM — zero-wait-state, directly coupled to Cortex-M55 fetch path
AXISRAM — 4.2 MB on-chip capacity, accessible by DMA and NPU AXI ports

pairs.left: Neural network model weights exceeding on-chip SRAM
pairs.right: XSPI memory-mapped external memory — appears as internal memory, supports NPU streaming weight fetch

pairs.left: Encrypted facial biometric templates at rest
pairs.right: MCE-encrypted XSPI region — inline encryption/decryption on every system bus access

micro_checks.explanation: Each allocation is driven by a different primary constraint: ITCM by latency, AXISRAM by capacity and DMA accessibility, XSPI memory-mapped by overflow beyond on-chip limits, and MCE-encrypted XSPI by the security requirement for inline protection of biometric data at rest.

content_gaps: The datasheet does not specify the maximum XSPI memory-mapped read bandwidth achievable in octal DTR mode at 200 MHz, which would be needed to verify that the NPU's weight fetch rate is satisfied without stalling inference.

The datasheet does not describe whether DTCM supports DMA access at all (only CPU-private access is implied by 'tightly coupled'), but this is not explicitly stated as a prohibition in the available chunks — a reference manual (RM0486) would be needed to confirm DMA accessibility rules for TCM regions.

The number and address ranges of individual AXISRAM banks (AXISRAM1 through AXISRAM6) are referenced in the learning objectives but not present in the available document chunks, preventing a precise bank-level allocation table from being constructed from this source alone.

stem.scenario_context: You are defining the memory layout for the STM32N6-based face recognition terminal. The inference engine requires ultra-low latency access to the real-time processing code that runs on every captured frame, and the neural network model weights are too large to fit in on-chip SRAM.
stem.question_text: Where should you allocate the time-critical inference control code and the neural network model weights, respectively, to achieve the lowest possible latency for the real-time face matching pipeline?

options.text: Inference control code in AXISRAM1; model weights in DTCM RAM — AXISRAM provides wide bus access and DTCM is tightly coupled to the core

Inference control code in ITCM RAM; model weights in XSPI-mapped external memory — ITCM is directly coupled to the Cortex-M55 fetch path and XSPI memory-mapped mode makes external flash appear as internal memory
Inference control code in DTCM RAM; model weights in AXISRAM6 — DTCM supports instruction fetches and AXISRAM6 offers the largest on-chip capacity for weight storage

feedback.correct: Correct. The 64-Kbyte instruction TCM RAM (ITCM) is specifically designed for critical real-time routines with zero-wait-state access from the Cortex-M55 fetch path. XSPI memory-mapped mode makes external flash appear as internal memory to the system, making it the right location for large model weights that cannot fit on-chip.
feedback.incorrect: DTCM RAM is optimized for data, not instruction fetches — placing executable code there is not the intended use. AXISRAM is a valid general-purpose buffer but lacks the zero-wait-state fetch path of ITCM. For model weights, XSPI memory-mapped mode is the correct choice because it presents external flash as addressable internal memory, which the NPU streaming engines can access efficiently. Review the datasheet Section 3.1 (TCM RAM) and Section 3.17 (XSPI memory-mapped mode).

references.snippet: The Cortex-M55 includes a 32-Kbyte ICACHE, a 32-Kbyte DCACHE, as well as 128-Kbyte data TCM RAM and 64-Kbyte instruction TCM RAM with ECC for critical real-time routines.
Memory-mapped mode: the external memory is memory mapped and it is seen by the system as if it was an internal memory, supporting both read and write operations.

stem.scenario_context: You are specifying the security architecture for the biometric terminal. The design stores encrypted facial templates in XSPI-connected external NOR flash. A colleague proposes using the STM32N6x7xx variant (which includes the Neural-ART NPU) to handle both inference and template storage security in a single device.
stem.question_text: You need to evaluate whether the STM32N6x7xx variant can satisfy the requirement for inline encryption of the external flash region holding biometric templates. What is the correct assessment?

options.text: The STM32N6x7xx can satisfy the requirement — the NPU subsystem includes its own real-time data encryption engine that can be redirected to protect the XSPI memory region

The STM32N6x7xx cannot satisfy the requirement — the MCE (Memory Cipher Engine), which provides system bus inline encryption for XSPI-connected memory, is available only on STM32N655xx and STM32N657xx devices
The STM32N6x7xx can satisfy the requirement — the XSPI interface includes its own cipher engine that operates independently of the MCE peripheral

feedback.correct: Correct. The MCE performs system bus inline encryption and decryption for defined memory regions, and it is explicitly restricted to STM32N655xx and STM32N657xx devices. The STM32N6x7xx variant (NPU line) does not include MCE, making it unable to provide transparent inline encryption of the XSPI-connected flash region where biometric templates are stored.
feedback.incorrect: The NPU's real-time encryption feature applies to model weight data flowing through the NPU pipeline — it is not a general-purpose memory region cipher engine. The XSPI interface does not contain an independent cipher engine equivalent to MCE. The MCE is the correct peripheral for inline encryption of external memory regions, and it is only available on the STM32N6x5xx (crypto) variants. Review datasheet Section 3.36 (MCE) and the variant feature comparison.

references.snippet: The memory cipher engine (MCE) defines, in a given address space, multiple regions with specific security setup (encryption). All system bus traffic going through an encrypted region is managed on-the-fly by the MCE, automatically decrypting reads and encrypting writes if authorized. This function is available only on STM32N655xx and STM32N657xx devices.

MCE main features are: System bus in-line encryption (for writes) and decryption (for reads), based on embedded firewall programming — Four encryption modes per region (maximum 4 regions available): no encryption (bypass mode), stream cipher, block cipher and fast block cipher modes

stem.scenario_context: You are reviewing the memory allocation plan for the face recognition terminal. The plan proposes storing active camera frame buffers — each approximately 1.5 MB for a 1080p raw Bayer frame — in the 128-Kbyte DTCM RAM to minimize DMA transfer latency to the ISP.
stem.question_text: You need to evaluate whether this allocation is feasible and identify the correct alternative if it is not.

options.text: The allocation is feasible — DTCM RAM supports DMA access and 128 Kbytes is sufficient for compressed frame data after ISP preprocessing
The allocation is not feasible — 128 Kbytes of DTCM is far smaller than a 1.5 MB frame buffer; AXISRAM (part of the 4.2 Mbyte on-chip SRAM) is the correct location for active frame buffers
The allocation is not feasible — DTCM RAM does not support DMA access at all; frame buffers must be placed in XSPI-mapped external memory regardless of size

feedback.correct: Correct. The DTCM RAM is only 128 Kbytes, which is far too small to hold a 1.5 MB camera frame buffer. DTCM is intended for critical real-time data variables, not large frame buffers. The 4.2 Mbyte on-chip SRAM (organized as AXISRAM1–6 and other banks) is the appropriate location for active frame buffers, providing sufficient capacity with ECC protection and high-bandwidth AXI bus access.
feedback.incorrect: A 1.5 MB frame buffer cannot fit in 128 Kbytes of DTCM regardless of compression — raw Bayer frames from a high-resolution camera sensor are inherently large. While XSPI external memory can hold frame data, it introduces higher latency than on-chip SRAM and should be reserved for model weights and encrypted biometric templates. AXISRAM is the correct on-chip destination for active frame buffers. Review datasheet Section 3.1 (TCM sizes) and Section 3.2 (SRAM configuration).

references.snippet: 4.2-Mbyte contiguous SRAM; 128-Kbyte TCM (tightly-coupled memory) RAM with ECC for critical real-time data + 64-Kbyte instruction TCM RAM with ECC for critical real-time routines
AHBSRAM1/2, AXISRAM1 to 6, BKPSRAM, FLEXRAM, and VENCRAM, with the following features: Error code correction (ECC): Single-error detection and correction with interrupt generation

stem.scenario_context: You are building the security feature mapping table for the biometric terminal. The terminal must protect biometric templates stored in external flash (data at rest) and verify the integrity of firmware images loaded at boot. You have selected the STM32N657xx variant.
stem.question_text: Which assignment of security peripherals to protection roles is correct for this design?

options.text: MCE for inline encryption of XSPI-connected external flash; HASH for firmware image integrity verification at boot; TrustZone for isolating the secure biometric processing domain
SAES for inline encryption of XSPI-connected external flash; PKA for firmware image integrity verification at boot; MCE for isolating the secure biometric processing domain
CRYP for inline encryption of XSPI-connected external flash; TrustZone for firmware image integrity verification at boot; HASH for isolating the secure biometric processing domain

feedback.correct: Correct. MCE is specifically designed for system bus inline encryption of memory regions — it transparently encrypts writes and decrypts reads on the XSPI-connected flash without CPU intervention. HASH provides cryptographic digest computation for data integrity verification such as firmware image validation. TrustZone partitions the processor into secure and non-secure worlds, making it the correct mechanism for isolating the biometric processing domain.
feedback.incorrect: SAES and CRYP are block cipher engines suited for encrypting discrete data payloads (such as biometric templates before storage), not for transparent inline memory region encryption — that role belongs to MCE. PKA is a public-key accelerator used for asymmetric operations like key exchange, not for hash-based integrity checks. TrustZone provides execution domain isolation, not data integrity verification. Review datasheet Sections 3.33–3.37 for the correct role of each security peripheral.

references.snippet: The memory cipher engine (MCE) defines, in a given address space, multiple regions with specific security setup (encryption). All system bus traffic going through an encrypted region is managed on-the-fly by the MCE, automatically decrypting reads and encrypting writes if authorized. This function is available only on STM32N655xx and STM32N657xx devices.

The SAES (available only on STM32N655xx and STM32N657xx devices) encrypts or decrypts data in compliance with the advanced encryption standard (AES) defined by NIST. It incorporates a protection against side-channel attacks (SCA), including differential power analysis (DPA), certified SESIP and PSA security assurance level 3.
